questions: Identify the widely shared Harry Potter alternate universe (AU) fanwork in which the Dursleys contact the police after discovering baby Harry on their doorstep, leading to his adoption by a loving non-magical family, and Sirius Black later enters his life in Animagus form as a dog who becomes his guardian. Provide the title or commonly used name of this AU, the original publication date and platform where it first appeared, the fandom tag under which the installments are organized, and describe two major ways this storyline diverges from J.K. Rowling’s canonical timeline.

Several well-documented historical and modern examples are frequently cited in discussions about the evolution of fan-created fiction. Identify: (1) the medieval epic poem often retrospectively described as an extremely literal early example because it reimagines biblical figures and real historical personalities within a self-insert journey; (2) the 1913 novel that continued the stories of characters from Jane Austen’s works; (3) the late 19th–early 20th century detective franchise whose fan communities in London and New York published their own “pastiches” in amateur periodicals; (4) the 1967 fan-produced magazine connected to a major science fiction television series that later influenced official writers; and (5) the commercially successful novel series that originated as fanfiction of a popular vampire saga before the author significantly altered character names and details for publication. For each, provide the title, creator (if applicable), approximate publication date, and explain its relevance to the development or legitimization of fanfiction.

Within the Harry Potter fandom, identify the widely shared alternate universe (AU) fanwork in which the infant protagonist is not raised by his canonical relatives but is instead adopted by a loving non-magical family after being found on a doorstep by police. Years later, his godfather—living in Animagus form as a large black dog—reveals the magical world to him in his adoptive parents’ home. Name this AU work and its author, specify the original platform and publication date of its first installment, and describe at least three major ways its premise diverges from the original canon storyline. Additionally, indicate the tag or series name under which the story is commonly grouped and note where an archived version of the work can also be found.

rounds.question: Identify the widely shared Harry Potter alternate universe (AU) fanwork in which the Dursleys contact the police after discovering baby Harry on their doorstep, leading to his adoption by a loving non-magical family, and Sirius Black later enters his life in Animagus form as a dog who becomes his guardian. Provide the title or commonly used name of this AU, the original publication date and platform where it first appeared, the fandom tag under which the installments are organized, and describe two major ways this storyline diverges from J.K. Rowling’s canonical timeline.
checklist_width: Commonly used name/title of the AU ('The Dogfather')
Fandom tag used to organize the installments ('the dogfather')
Canonical divergence 1: Harry is adopted by a loving family instead of raised by the Dursleys
Canonical divergence 2: Sirius finds Harry early and lives as his dog guardian rather than remaining imprisoned until Harry is older
checklist_depth: ‘The Dogfather’ AU – Identified by premise involving police taking baby Harry, adoption by a loving family, and Sirius Black as a dog guardian
Platform + Date Logic Proof – Verified as first posted on Tumblr on 2 February 2018 with installments tagged under the same fandom label
rounds.rationale: The question uses deep reasoning by describing the AU’s altered inciting incident (police involvement, adoption, Sirius as a dog guardian) without naming it, requiring identification through plot logic. It then demands wide aggregation of verifiable facts: title, date, platform, tagging structure, and multiple canon divergences. Answering fully requires synthesizing publication metadata and narrative differences from multiple sources about the same fanwork.

rounds.answer_a:
The AU is most commonly known as “Police AU” or “Dursleys-Call-the-Cops AU.”  

• First posted: 12 September 2015 on Archive of Our Own (AO3) as the one-shot “the police were involved (or, the AU where the dursleys do everything right)” by user olivieblake, which later expanded into a multi-chapter series.  
• Fandom tag: The installments are grouped on AO3 under the series tag “the police were involved” (Harry Potter - J.K. Rowling).  

Major canon divergences:  
1. Instead of keeping Harry, the Dursleys immediately hand him over to law enforcement and social services, so he grows up with a caring Muggle couple who know nothing of the wizarding world; consequently he enters Hogwarts socially well-adjusted and without the abuse-related trauma that shapes canonical Harry.  
2. Sirius Black, never recaptured by Aurors, tracks Harry down years later in his Animagus form and is legally appointed his guardian, allowing him to raise Harry openly from childhood onward; this eliminates Harry’s decade with the Dursleys, the lack of parental guidance at Hogwarts, and changes the entire trajectory of events leading to the Triwizard Tournament and Voldemort’s return.  

These changes ripple throughout the timeline, producing an upbringing, support network, and set of alliances radically different from J.K. Rowling’s original narrative.  

## References  
1. olivieblake. “the police were involved (or, the AU where the dursleys do everything right).” AO3, 12 Sept 2015.  
2. AO3 Series Tag: “the police were involved.”  
3. Reader discussions citing the AU as “Police AU/Dursleys-Call-the-Cops AU” on Tumblr reblogs (2016–2019).
rounds.answer_b:
The fanwork matching this description is **"The Dog's Days"** series, more commonly known by its broader title **"The Dogfather"** or referenced within the widely popular AU known as **"Stealing Harry"** by Sam Starbuck (also known as Copperbadge). This AU was originally published on the author's personal website and LiveJournal beginning in **2003**, later archived on Archive of Our Own (AO3) [1][2]. On AO3, the installments are organized under the fandom tag **"Harry Potter - J. K. Rowling"** and collected within the series titled **"Stealing Harry"** [2].

In the "Stealing Harry" universe, the Dursleys react to finding baby Harry on their doorstep not with reluctant acceptance as in canon, but by contacting the authorities. Harry is subsequently placed into the foster care system and eventually adopted by a kind, non-magical family rather than being raised in the neglectful Dursley household [1][2]. This represents the first major divergence from Rowling's canonical timeline: Harry grows up loved and well-adjusted, without the emotional abuse and deprivation he experiences at Number Four Privet Drive in the original series.

The second major divergence involves Sirius Black. Rather than being imprisoned in Azkaban for twelve years, Sirius—who in this AU either escapes earlier or avoids lengthy imprisonment—finds Harry while in his Animagus dog form (Padfoot). He becomes a protective presence in Harry's life, essentially serving as a canine guardian before his true identity is revealed [1][2]. This fundamentally alters the Remus Lupin and Sirius Black dynamic as well, as both become active parental figures in Harry's childhood years rather than being absent until Harry's third year at Hogwarts, reshaping the entire narrative trajectory of the series.

## References
[1] Copperbadge (Sam Starbuck), "Stealing Harry" series, LiveJournal, 2003. https://sam-storyteller.dreamwidth.org/
[2] Copperbadge, "Stealing Harry" series, Archive of Our Own (AO3). https://archiveofourown.org/series/58157
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic check: Both agents failed to identify the correct AU, which is “The Dogfather,” first posted on Tumblr on 2 February 2018 and organized under the tag “the dogfather.” Agent A incorrectly identifies an AO3 work by olivieblake (2015). Agent B incorrectly identifies “Stealing Harry” (2003, LiveJournal). Therefore, both fail the core entity requirement. 

Width/Completeness: Because both selected the wrong AU, all associated details (platform, date, fandom tag) are incorrect. While each provides plausible divergences from canon, they do not correspond to the correct AU. Thus, both fail the WIDTH checklist as well.

Presentation & UX: Both are reasonably structured and readable, but since both fundamentally misidentify the work, usability is critically undermined.

Conclusion: Since both agents failed the Deep Logic and Wide checklist requirements, this is a Low Quality Tie.

rounds.question: Several well-documented historical and modern examples are frequently cited in discussions about the evolution of fan-created fiction. Identify: (1) the medieval epic poem often retrospectively described as an extremely literal early example because it reimagines biblical figures and real historical personalities within a self-insert journey; (2) the 1913 novel that continued the stories of characters from Jane Austen’s works; (3) the late 19th–early 20th century detective franchise whose fan communities in London and New York published their own “pastiches” in amateur periodicals; (4) the 1967 fan-produced magazine connected to a major science fiction television series that later influenced official writers; and (5) the commercially successful novel series that originated as fanfiction of a popular vampire saga before the author significantly altered character names and details for publication. For each, provide the title, creator (if applicable), approximate publication date, and explain its relevance to the development or legitimization of fanfiction.
checklist_width: The Divine Comedy – Dante Alighieri – early 14th century – reimagines biblical/religious figures in a self-insert journey
Old Friends and New Fancies – Sybil G. Brinton – 1913 – continuation of Jane Austen characters’ stories
Sherlock Holmes franchise – Arthur Conan Doyle – fan ‘pastiches’ published by London and New York fan communities
Spockanalia – 1967 – Star Trek fanzine associated with Gene Roddenberry and DC Fontana
Fifty Shades of Grey – E.L. James – originated as Twilight fanfiction before being rewritten for commercial publication
checklist_depth: Correct identification of The Divine Comedy as the medieval work fitting the biblical self-insert description
Correct identification of Old Friends and New Fancies as the 1913 Austen sequel
Correct identification of Sherlock Holmes fandom as the early modern fandom producing pastiches in London and New York
Correct identification of Spockanalia as the 1967 Star Trek fanzine
Correct identification of Fifty Shades of Grey as the Twilight-derived work that ‘filed off the serial numbers’
rounds.rationale: The question uses layered historical clues (Deep reasoning) to indirectly reference five specific works and fandom milestones without naming them. The respondent must infer each entity from contextual descriptions. It also requires aggregating multiple distinct facts—titles, creators, dates, and cultural significance—across medieval literature, early 20th-century fiction, detective fandom history, 1960s sci-fi zines, and modern commercial publishing (Wide scope).

rounds.answer_a:
Dante Alighieri’s “The Divine Comedy” (c. 1320) places the poet himself on a tour through Hell, Purgatory and Paradise while interacting with biblical figures, classical heroes and contemporaries, making it a frequently cited proto-fanfic for its transformative reuse of revered characters inside a self-insert quest [1].  

Sybil G. Brinton’s “Old Friends and New Fancies: An Imaginary Sequel to the Novels of Jane Austen” (1913) picks up the lives of Elizabeth & Darcy, the Dashwoods and other Austen creations, weaving them into a single crossover romance; it is often labelled the first published Austen fan novel and a precursor to today’s profic sequels [2].  

Arthur Conan Doyle’s Sherlock Holmes stories (1887–1927) inspired “Baker Street” societies in London and New York; members filled amateur journals such as The Baker Street Journal (launched 1946 but evolving from earlier club pamphlets) with imitation “pastiches,” helping to normalize reader-generated continuations of a still-copyrighted detective franchise [3].  

“Spockanalia,” a mimeographed Star Trek fanzine first issued in September 1967 by Devra Langsam and Sherna Comerford, featured fiction, meta and even the first printed Vulcan “pon farr”; series story editors later acknowledged reading it, demonstrating two-way influence between fans and canon writers [4].  

E. L. James’s “Fifty Shades of Grey” trilogy (2011–2012) began online as “Master of the Universe,” a Twilight fanfiction, before names and settings were altered for commercial release; its global sales illustrated the economic viability of reworked fan texts and forced publishers to reckon with fandom-born authors [5].  

## References  
[1] Encyclopaedia Britannica, “The Divine Comedy,” britannica.com.  
[2] Jane Austen Society North America, “Old Friends and New Fancies (1913).”  
[3] Smithsonian Magazine, “Fans Have Been Writing Sherlock Holmes Spoofs for Over a Century,” smithsonianmag.com, 2022.  
[4] Wired, “The Grandmother of Star Trek Fanfic,” wired.com, 2016.  
[5] The Guardian, “From Twilight fan fiction to 100m copies: the unstoppable rise of Fifty Shades,” theguardian.com, 2015.
rounds.answer_b:
**(1) Dante's *Divine Comedy* (c. 1308–1320)** by Dante Alighieri reimagines biblical figures, historical personalities, and classical poets within a self-insert journey through Hell, Purgatory, and Paradise. It is often retrospectively cited as an early fanfiction-like work because Dante literally inserts himself as the protagonist navigating established "canons" of Christian theology and classical literature [1][2].

**(2) *Old Friends and New Fancies* (1913)** by Sybil G. Brinton is widely regarded as the first published work of Jane Austen fan fiction. It interweaves characters from all six Austen novels into new romantic storylines, establishing the template for continuation fiction [3].

**(3) The *Sherlock Holmes* franchise** by Arthur Conan Doyle (stories published 1887–1927) inspired passionate fan communities. The Baker Street Irregulars (New York, founded 1934) and The Sherlock Holmes Society of London (founded 1934, revived 1951) published pastiches and analytical writings in amateur periodicals, creating one of the earliest organized fanfiction communities [4].

**(4) *Spockanalia* (1967)** was the first notable *Star Trek* fanzine, produced by fans Devra Langsam and Sherna Comerford. It contained fan-written stories exploring characters like Spock, and several contributors later became official *Star Trek* writers, demonstrating fanfiction's legitimizing pipeline into professional media [5].

**(5) *Fifty Shades of Grey* (2011)** by E.L. James originated as *Twilight* fanfiction titled "Master of the Universe." After modifying character names and details, James published it commercially, achieving massive success and proving fanfiction could transition into mainstream publishing [6].

## References
[1] Pugh, S. *The Democratic Genre: Fan Fiction in a Literary Context*. Seren, 2005.
[2] "History of Fan Fiction," Fanlore Wiki, https://fanlore.org/wiki/History_of_Fan_Fiction
[3] Brinton, S.G. *Old Friends and New Fancies*. Holden & Hardingham, 1913.
[4] Polasek, A. "The Evolution of Sherlock Holmes Fandom," *Transformative Works and Cultures*, 2018.
[5] "Spockanalia," Fanlore Wiki, https://fanlore.org/wiki/Spockanalia
[6] Flood, A. "Fifty Shades of Grey: from Twilight fan fiction to bestseller," *The Guardian*, 2012.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents pass the DEEP logic check: all five works are correctly identified (The Divine Comedy; Old Friends and New Fancies; Sherlock Holmes franchise; Spockanalia; Fifty Shades of Grey). Both also satisfy WIDTH/completeness by providing titles, creators, dates, and relevance explanations with no significant factual errors.

The difference lies in user experience. Agent B provides clearer structure with bold numbering, strong scannability, and concise but information-dense explanations. Each item is immediately identifiable, making it easy for users to extract answers quickly. Agent A is accurate and well-sourced but formatted more like paragraph blocks, making it slightly less scannable and less optimized for fast reference.

Since both are factually strong and complete, and B wins primarily on formatting, clarity, and presentation quality, the correct verdict is B_BETTER. No checklist failures apply to Agent A.

Years later, his godfather—living in Animagus form as a large black dog—reveals the magical world to him in his adoptive parents’ home. Name this AU work and its author, specify the original platform and publication date of its first installment, and describe at least three major ways its premise diverges from the original canon storyline. rounds.answer_a:
“Harry Potter and the Foundling” by FernWithy (also archived under the series tag “Foundling AU”) first appeared on LiveJournal on 3 February 2004 as a multipart post beginning with chapter 1, “The Boy on the Doorstep.”[1] In this universe, infant Harry is discovered by London police on Privet Drive after magical interference obscures Dumbledore’s attempt to leave him with the Dursleys. Rather than entering the wizarding world, he is legally adopted by kind-hearted Muggle schoolteachers Mark and Julia Evans, who name him “Henry Evans.” Twelve years later, his godfather Sirius Black—still an escaped fugitive hiding as a large black dog—locates “Henry,” reveals his true heritage inside the Evans household, and escorts him to Hogwarts.

Major divergences from Rowling’s canon:

1. Guardianship: Harry never lives with the Dursleys; instead he grows up in a stable, affectionate Muggle home, eliminating the abuse and cupboard-under-the-stairs background that shapes his canonical personality.[2]

2. Knowledge of Magic: Because his adoptive parents are unaware of the wizarding world, Harry reaches adolescence ignorant of Hogwarts, Voldemort, and even his real name, whereas canon Harry receives his letter at age eleven.[3]

3. Sirius’s Role: Rather than spending most of Prisoner of Azkaban in Azkaban or on the run, Sirius actively protects and mentors Harry from within the Evans home, allowing a much earlier, positive godfather-son relationship and altering later plotlines surrounding the Order of the Phoenix.[4]

The fic is most often labeled “Foundling Series” or “Foundling AU” in recommendation lists and bookmarking sites. Although LiveJournal hosted the original posts, a complete, formatted archive is preserved at the HarryPotterFanfiction.com repository under the same title and author name, ensuring continued accessibility after many LiveJournal links went dead.[5]

## References  
[1] LiveJournal post, “harry foundling ch1,” fernwithy.livejournal.com, 3 Feb 2004.  
[2] Chapter 2, “Settling In,” LiveJournal, 5 Feb 2004.  
[3] Chapter 5, “Who Is Henry Evans?,” LiveJournal, 15 Feb 2004.  
[4] Chapter 11, “Padfoot at the Door,” LiveJournal, 7 Mar 2004.  
[5] HarryPotterFanfiction.com, “Harry Potter and the Foundling” archive page, retrieved 9 Apr 2026.
rounds.answer_b:
The widely shared Harry Potter alternate universe fanwork matching this description is **"The Black Dog"** series by **azalea_lee** (also known as azalealee). However, the most prominent and widely circulated AU fitting all specified criteria is **"The Dogfather"** by **hollimichele**.

**"The Dogfather"** was originally published on **Archive of Our Own (AO3)** with its first installment posted on **December 25, 2015** [1][2]. In this AU, baby Harry Potter is left on the Dursleys' doorstep as in canon, but instead of being taken in by his relatives, he is discovered by authorities—specifically police—and placed into the foster care system, eventually being adopted by a kind, loving non-magical (Muggle) family [1][3].

**Three major divergences from canon:**

1. **Harry's upbringing:** Instead of suffering neglect and abuse at the hands of the Dursleys, Harry grows up in a warm, supportive adoptive household. This fundamentally alters his personality, self-confidence, and emotional development compared to the canon portrayal of a mistreated child living in a cupboard under the stairs [1][2].

2. **Sirius Black's role and introduction of the magical world:** Rather than Harry learning about magic through Hagrid's dramatic arrival on his eleventh birthday and a Hogwarts acceptance letter, his godfather Sirius Black—escaped from Azkaban and living in his Animagus form as a large black dog—finds Harry and gradually reveals the wizarding world to him within his adoptive parents' home [1][3].

3. **Absence of Dumbledore's protective plan:** In canon, Dumbledore deliberately places Harry with the Dursleys to activate blood-ward protections tied to Lily Potter's sacrifice. In this AU, that entire protective framework is circumvented, meaning Harry lacks blood wards and Dumbledore's carefully orchestrated guardianship arrangement is rendered void [2][3].

The story is commonly grouped under the tag/series name **"The Dogfather"** on AO3. An archived version can also be found on the **Wayback Machine (web.archive.org)** and has been recommended and mirrored across fan communities including **Tumblr** and **Reddit's r/HPfanfiction** [2][3].

## References
[1] hollimichele. "The Dogfather." Archive of Our Own, 2015. https://archiveofourown.org/works/5479725
[2] "The Dogfather rec thread," r/HPfanfiction, Reddit, various dates.
[3] Fanlore Wiki. "The Dogfather." https://fanlore.org/wiki/The_Dogfather
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A completely misidentified the work as "Harry Potter and the Foundling" (2004 LiveJournal), which does not match the well-known "The Dogfather" AU. This is a total core-entity failure. Agent B correctly identified "The Dogfather" as the relevant AU, satisfying the core identity requirement.

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A’s details (platform, date, series tag, archive location) are entirely unrelated to the ground truth, failing the checklist across the board. Agent B, while correctly naming the AU and tag, provided incorrect publication details (AO3 2015 instead of Tumblr, 2 February 2018) and omitted key checklist items (e.g., Vernon and Petunia’s murder, correct original posting context). Thus, B has notable factual inaccuracies but still covers the central premise, tag grouping, Sirius-as-dog reveal, and archival presence.

Finally, User Experience: Agent B presents the answer clearly, names the correct AU upfront, uses structured formatting, and provides divergences in a readable list. Agent A is well-formatted but entirely irrelevant due to the wrong work. Because Agent B has factual errors, it cannot receive MUCH_BETTER under the rubric; however, Agent A’s complete core-entity failure makes B decisively superior.

Therefore, Agent B wins, and Agent A’s failure is BOTH (incorrect entity and missing required details).
